content:
What I Learned About System Design (Part 8)

4 min read
Just now

--

Why caching exists in the first place
Your computer has two broad types of memory: volatile and non-volatile. Non-volatile memory like an SSD, keeps its data even if the power goes out. RAM is the opposite: it loses everything the moment power’s gone. But RAM is dramatically faster to read from and write to than an SSD.
So here’s the setup: your actual data lives in a database, which is ultimately backed by something like an SSD or disk. That’s reliable, but reading from it repeatedly, for the same data, over and over, is slow compared to what RAM can do. A cache sits in front of that, a smaller, faster layer, usually living in RAM, that holds onto the data you’re likely to ask for again soon, so you don’t have to go all the way back to the database every single time.
Think about an app like Instagram. Some posts get viewed by a handful of people. Say, something a celebrity posted get viewed millions of times within an hour. Fetching that same popular post from the database on every single view would be wasteful; instead, you keep it sitting in the cache, ready to serve instantly, and only go back to the database occasionally.
So what is a cache, really
A cache is short term memory with limited space, sitting between your application and your actual data source, and it’s faster to read from than that original source.
Cache invalidation: knowing when data’s gone stale
Here’s the problem that comes with keeping a copy of something: what happens when the original changes? If the database gets updated but the cache still has the old version sitting there, anyone reading from the cache gets outdated data without knowing it.
Cache invalidation is the strategy for handling this, some way of marking cached data as outdated once it no longer matches the source, so the system knows to go fetch a fresh copy from the database instead of confidently handing out something stale.
The three write policies
This is where it gets genuinely interesting, because each of these makes a different tradeoff between speed and correctness.
Write-through — the write goes to both the cache and the database at the same time, and it’s only considered successful once both succeed. Think of a bank withdrawal: if you take money out, that has to be reflected in the actual database, not just sitting in a cache that might vanish. So the system waits on both writes before telling you “done.” This is slower per write, but that’s the tradeoff you want when correctness matters more than speed.
Write-around — you skip the cache on write entirely, and only populate it later, if and when the data actually gets read. Think of uploading a YouTube video: there’s no reason to shove a fresh upload straight into the cache most videos aren’t watched by many people. Only once a video starts actually getting views (or goes viral) does it make sense to cache it. Writing straight to cache on every upload would just fill it with things nobody’s asking for.
Write-back — the write goes to the cache first, and the user gets an instant “success” response, while the actual database write happens afterward, asynchronously. Think of liking a post: network latency between client and server is never zero, but a like needs to feel instant. So the system updates the cache immediately, tells you it worked, and quietly syncs that change to the database a moment later. Fast for the user, at the cost of a brief window where the cache and the database don’t perfectly agree.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cache eviction: what happens when the cache fills up
A cache has limited space, so at some point, something has to be removed to make room for something new. A few common policies for deciding what gets evicted:

• LRU (Least Recently Used) — evict whatever hasn’t been accessed in the longest time. The idea: if nobody’s touched it in a while, it’s probably not needed right now either.

• LFU (Least Frequently Used) — evict whatever has been accessed the fewest number of times overall, not just recently.

• FIFO (First In, First Out) — evict whatever was added to the cache earliest, regardless of how often it’s been used since.

• Round Robin — cycle through evicting entries in a fixed rotation, rather than basing it on usage at all.
LRU and LFU are the two you’ll run into constantly in real systems — LRU in particular is extremely common as a default. FIFO and Round Robin show up too, though usually in simpler or more specialized cases.
Global cache vs. distributed cache
Global cache is the simple version: one single cache, usually sitting alongside your server. Straightforward, but it’s a single point of failure, if that cache goes down or gets wiped, everything cached is just gone, and every request has to fall back to the database until it warms back up.
Distributed cache spreads the cache across multiple nodes instead of relying on one. And deciding which node holds which piece of cached data brings back the exact same idea from Part 3, consistent hashing. Each cache node sits on the ring, and a given key gets hashed to figure out which node should hold it, for all the same reasons a distributed database or a load balancer uses the same trick: even distribution, and only a small, localized disruption if one node goes down, instead of the whole cache falling apart at once.
That’s it for this part. I’d love to hear what surprised you about it in the comments.
